A Guide to Painting Rendered Walls Rendering refers to the process of applying a coat of cement on the external walls of a property to make them smooth or textured as desired. The difference between rendering and plastering is that rendering involves the exterior walls while plastering involves the interior ones. Traditionally, rendered walls are built up in two or three coats, which makes it less likely that surface cracks will develop.

Time: 20 minutes Cost: $15 for turpentine and beeswax Difficulty: Difficult Preservation of original colour: High Beeswax, when mixed with turpentine, creates a paste that can add body to your oil-based paint, and heres how to do it: Start by grating a block of pure beeswax into a container. The amount of beeswax required will depend on the volume of paint you need to thicken, but a good starting point is a quarter of the total volume of the paint.

Next, pour turpentine into the container until the grated beeswax is just covered. Turpentine acts as a solvent in this mixture, breaking down the beeswax and helping it blend smoothly with the paint. Set the container in a larger pan filled with boiling water to create a makeshift double boiler. The Colour Changing Phenomenon of Paint The illusion of darkening or lightening as paint dries is a curious phenomenon influenced by multiple factors. When paint is wet, it often appears darker due to reduced light reflection caused by the wetting agents and solvents. But as it dries, the water and solvents evaporate, resulting in increased light reflection and a potential lightening effect.

Higher gloss levels tend to reflect more light and can make the paint appear brighter, while lower gloss levels can result in a darker appearance due to reduced light reflection. Paint Application. The thickness and evenness of the paint application can also influence the lightness or darkness of the dried paint. The porosity of the surface being painted can affect drying times. Highly porous surfaces tend to absorb solvents, which can extend drying times. Curing: Understanding the Full Picture While its tempting to think paint is good to go as soon as it feels dry to the touch, theres more to the story. Heres the key difference between drying and curing: Drying: This is the initial stage where the paints liquid components, primarily water or solvents, evaporate. The paint film feels dry to the touch, but the curing process hasnt yet begun. Curing: This is a much slower chemical process where the paint transforms.

Imagine tiny paint particles like marbles scattered across the surface. During drying, the water evaporates, leaving the paint particles close together. But something more needs to happen for a truly strong and durable paint film. Coalescence is like the paint particles merging to hold hands. These hands are binders in the paint that act like glue. When the binders connect the paint particles, they form a solid film thats resistant to scratches, stains, and washing.

Roller Nap Lengths for Different Door Textures The texture of your door should dictate the nap length of your roller. For doors with a flat surface, a shorter nap of about 1/4 inch to 3/8 inch will suffice, ensuring a smooth application without any unwanted texture. Finish with a fine grit: Finally, use a fine-grit sandpaper (220-240 grit) to achieve a smooth finish. Durability and Protective Performance Adhesion Characteristics Brushed paint exhibits 20–30% stronger adhesion (measured via ASTM D4541 pull-off tests) due to mechanical keying into substrate irregularities. Sprayed coatings rely more on chemical adhesion, making them prone to delamination on chalky or glossy surfaces without proper priming.

This method creates a thin, even layer that conforms to textured substrates such as stucco or clapboard siding. Modern airless sprayers operate at pressures exceeding 3,000 psi, enabling efficient coverage of 300–400 square feet per hour. However, the process demands meticulous masking of adjacent surfaces to contain overspray, which can account for 20–30% of paint waste due to wind drift and rebound effects.

Brushing remains economically favourable for single-use DIY applications, with total project costs 15–20% lower than spraying in small-scale scenarios. Aesthetic and Functional Outcomes Surface Finish Quality Spraying produces factory-like uniformity on smooth surfaces like HardiePlank or metal siding, eliminating brush marks. Textured surfaces benefit from spray’s ability to penetrate grooves unreachable by brushes.

Conversely, brushed finishes on wood substrates enhance perceived craftsmanship through visible grain highlighting. Coverage Uniformity Brush application achieves 98–99% first-pass coverage on porous surfaces versus 85–90% for spraying, necessitating additional spray coats. Film build consistency varies ±0. 2 mils with spraying versus ±0. 5 mils with brushing under controlled conditions.

Labour Intensity and Physical Demand Brush painting imposes greater ergonomic strain, necessitating repetitive arm motions and ladder repositioning. Sprayers reduce physical exertion but require sustained trigger control and equipment handling (6–8 lb units for residential models). Economic Analysis Equipment and Material Costs Consumer-grade airless sprayers range from $300–$800, versus $20–$50 for premium brushes.

Mechanics of Brush Painting The bristles work the paint into the tiny pores of the material, creating a strong bond, especially on rough surfaces like weathered wood or brick. Angled brushes are great for detail work, while wider brushes cover flat areas more quickly (about 100-150 square feet per hour). Brushing is slower than spraying and can leave brush marks on smooth surfaces.

Weather Resistance Field studies show brushed acrylic latex survives 8–12 years in temperate climates versus 6–10 years for sprayed equivalents, attributed to thicker dry film builds (4. 5 vs. 3. 2 mils). Both methods perform comparably when maintained with equivalent recoating schedules.

Efficiency and Time Considerations Project Scale and Time Investment Spray systems demonstrate clear temporal advantages for large-scale projects. A two-story home requiring 40 labour hours with brushes can be sprayed in 12–15 hours post-preparation. However, prep time for spraying exceeds brushing by 30–50% due to masking requirements for windows, fixtures, and landscaping. For sub-1,000 square foot projects, brushing often proves quicker overall by avoiding extensive setup.

Spray projects consume 25–40% more paint due to overspray and thinner application layers (2–3 mils vs. 4–5 mils for brushing). A 2,500 square foot home requires approximately 55 litres for spray versus 38 litres for brush application. Long-Term Cost Implications Despite higher initial costs, spray systems offer cost-efficiency for repeat projects or multi-home contractors through time savings.

Practical Considerations Surface Suitability Spray Preferred : Vinyl siding, stucco, large flat walls Brush Preferred : Decorative trim, weathered wood, intricate millwork Environmental and Safety Factors Brush application emits 15–20% fewer VOCs due to reduced overspray and aerosolisation.
